data.title: Electrovaya: Gathering Momentum And Pull Through Demand
data.description:
Electrovaya (NASDAQ:ELVA) is gaining momentum, revenue is increasing and profitability has arrived. They continue to deliver the longest cycle life - highest safety profile lithium ion battery, and their biggest end users, five Fortune 100 and 500 companies, have asked them to move into new vertical markets.

The pull-through demand from these committed long-term repeat Electrovaya battery buyers almost guarantees sales in the new verticals, providing Electrovaya with a launch pad to multiple years of growth.

I am long Electovaya, and it is one of my largest positions. I maintain my Strong Buy recommendation and am increasing my price target from $9.60 to $14. I will look to buy additional shares in any pullback.

Due Diligence: I interviewed the CEO of Electrovaya, Dr. Raj Gupta, on August 14th, and much of this article is based on the information received in that interview. It is the fourth time I have spoken with Dr. Gupta; he is extremely knowledgeable about his company and his industry. I have found him to be honest and precise with his words. He has never overpromised and does not beat around the bush.

I have covered Electrovaya multiple times, and this article is an update on the company's progress, concentrating on its potential in the coming years.

Sentiment Shifts

Sentiment counts for a great deal when it comes to the share price of emerging technology companies. If the general market is confident, the share price will often rise, and the market's confidence feeds off the performance of the company and its management.

With Electrovaya, a positive reinforcing loop is being established that could drive ongoing price appreciation. Electrovaya has begun to pick up Wall Street coverage as it has increased its revenue and moved towards profitability. The change in coverage from the date of my first article on Electrovaya is shown in this table.

Momentum is gathering: Raymond James initiated with a strong buy rating in January, Roth increased its price target to $5 in February, and this month, HC Wainwright maintained its $10 price target.

Company Improvements

The key change is how increased revenue and gross margins have led to profitability and positive cash flow. A key inflection point in the life of any business has been passed.

Future Revenue Growth

I believe Electrovaya is at the beginning of a multi-year revenue growth cycle that could deliver 2027 revenue double my previous 2025 forecast of $63 million.

This revenue growth will be delivered from the existing materials handling business and the opening of new vertical markets where Electrovaya's USP has value.

Existing Customers are driving Electrovaya

Electrovaya’s existing business: Supplying large batteries to be used in Toyota forklift trucks accounts for more than 80% of its revenue.

Electrovaya has five large end-user customers; they have never confirmed who they are. However, their largest customer is described as “A leading Fortune E-commerce company.” This customer has deployed over 2,500 batteries in 40 distribution centers, and Electrovaya regularly announces repeat orders. (The Fortune 100 list has only one company that could be described as an e-commerce company with operations across North America, Amazon.)

In 2022 (FY 2022), Electrovaya was under less scrutiny; they described their largest customers as Walmart and Mondelez. They have also mentioned repeat orders from Home Depot and Mars.

The five large companies are driving revenue in two ways: firstly, they are increasing the volumes of the existing materials handling products they are buying. The CEO noted in our conversation that these customers are now moving existing facilities to Electrovaya batteries as well as using them in new build facilities.

Secondly, and perhaps most importantly, they are providing pull-through demand, moving Electrovaya into new verticals, almost guaranteeing success.

Battery Storage

In my interview, I discussed this point at length, and the CEO told me that three of the big five customers have asked Electrovaya to prepare proposals to site battery storage solutions at their existing sites. The largest wants to put these containerized battery systems close to data centers (told you it was Amazon) and has provided guidance on the likely volume and sites for 2026.

The CEO told me that Electrovaya does not have to pay for the development of this new product; customers are funding the research, and it would not require any significant increase in staff to fulfill the orders.

This pull-through demand is a confirmation of the belief these companies have in Electrovaya's technology.

The batteries have two selling points: increased safety and increased life cycles.

For large companies, putting these batteries in their warehouse and adjacent to their facilities, safety and life cycle are the two most important specs. They need long-term reliability, high safety, and a good return on investment.

Electrovaya has proven its ability to meet these criteria for these large customers, and they are buying more as a result.

I do not have information about the revenue Electrovaya expects from this vertical. It is an enormous market, and with confirmed marquee customers and an outstanding safety record, Electrovaya can be expected to develop a significant market share.

The market is expected to grow with a CAGR of 15% between 2025 and 2034, with North America seeing the fastest growth but Asia the largest market overall. North America could see $3.5 billion in sales by 2034. A market share of 2% would double Electrovaya's current revenue.

Robots

Warehouse robots are an attractive market for battery companies, as they operate indoors and in large numbers, making them a potentially high-revenue sector.

Electrovaya announced contracts with three OEM robot manufacturers, all of which make robots that work inside warehouses. The CEO confirmed these verticals also have pull-through demand from the same large customers. Once more, customers are paying for the development of these products.

Robot batteries are much smaller than those supplied for materials handling and feature wireless charging; they have the same safety profile and life cycle specifications.

It means these robots can operate autonomously 24 hours a day, going through multiple wireless recharges each day, and the long life-cycle means the batteries will probably outlast the robots' moving parts.

Deliveries will begin later this year to the three OEMs, and despite smaller batteries (hence lower revenue per battery), it is likely to be a considerable revenue generator in 2026 and 2027.

The total market here is forecast to be even bigger than the energy storage market, expected to hit $85 billion by 2033, with North America expected to be the largest single market. 2% market share in North America will likely give revenue of $680 million (not a forecast, just a total addressable market projection). It would be a tenfold increase in Electrovaya revenue.

Again, Electrovaya has not had to pay for the R&D work to develop these batteries; they have guaranteed initial customers and can move immediately to scaled production. Pull-through demand is a wonder.

48V Batteries

Electrovaya sells a line of 48V batteries, necessary for applications that require large amounts of power. They are being integrated into three or four different vertical markets. The high-voltage batteries feature the same cells and proven life cycle/safety profile.

Earth Moving Equipment

Through Electrovaya's ongoing relationship with the Japanese trading house Sumitomo, Electrovaya is supplying two OEMs in this area. This application makes sense when the equipment is working underground (reduced fumes) or in city centers (reduced noise). Further shipments are expected next quarter; at present, it is a small sector.

It is difficult to predict if these electronic earth movers will sell in any volume or if they will be more of a niche product, providing Electrovaya with incremental revenue. The contract has passed SOP, so batteries being delivered are for production machines, not testing.

Class 8 Trucks

Electrovaya has a contract with Janus to provide batteries for their “battery swap” service. Again, there is logic behind this; the Australian company Janus provides long-distance electric class 8 trucks, mostly for mining and resource extraction. They have built battery swapping stations along the route, and rather than use diesel trucks to deliver diesel for the trucks to use, they are charging batteries with electricity generated on site.

Janus is also operating in California; I suspect this will be another niche application providing incremental revenue. I don't know if electric class 8 trucks will ever be a major market, but the basic idea seems sound and could fit in with long-distance autonomous trucking at some stage in the future.

Airport Ground Vehicles

Another recently announced vertical is the vehicles that push big jets around when they are on the ground. I am less clear on the logic here. I understand the industry wants to reduce its carbon footprint, but changing the ground vehicles when the airlines are burning jet fuel seems like a bit of window dressing.

It will never make a significant difference to the pollution this industry causes; I struggle to find a logical reason for it to exist. These machines work out in the open in very noisy environments close to huge amounts of fuel. The benefits of electric are not as clear as they are in the applications of earth-moving trucks or class 8 trucks. It will provide additional niche revenue, but at the moment, I can't really see an important selling point.

Defense

Electrovaya has signaled “an aggressive pursuit” of this market (CEO Q3 earnings prepared remarks).

Electrovaya is working with a defense contractor on a “mission-critical vehicle.” The sector is a high-growth one that the benefits of Electrovaya are well-suited to.

The made-in-America cells will be crucial for this project to ramp up. Management has guided to a pick up of orders in 2027 to coincide with US cell production (see manufacturing growth below).

The defense contractor is taking ongoing small volumes of the HV batteries. ELVA believes significant US defense business will come online with the production of cells in the US.

Trains

Last year, Electrovaya was working with a company deploying a battery-powered train on a private network. This project seems to have ended with its government grant, and I do not believe this vertical is moving forward at the moment.

Technology

Electrovay's Infinity batteries are Lithium Ion batteries consisting of an NMC cathode, a graphite anode, a ceramic separator, and liquid electrolyte.

The design is effectively fixed, and no major changes are expected to the chemistry apart from incremental improvements.

The advantage of the Infinity battery is derived from its ceramic separator, a patent-protected proprietary technology that Electrovaya acquired during a 2020 takeover (see earlier article). The separator gives the battery an extraordinary industry-leading cycle life and safety profile.

The ceramic separator is the technology on which the company stands; it provides a clear performance advantage over all of its competitors and provides a wide moat around the business.

Electrovaya announced (Q3 earnings) an upgrade to the separator that brings a new set of advantages, new patents, and improved operational efficiencies.

The new separator is made with an improved ceramic material with better thermal capabilities, is less expensive to manufacture, and crucially will be thinner and lighter, allowing Electrovaya to increase its battery density. Electrovaya will be able to bring the manufacturing of this separator in house or use local contract manufacturers, allowing for increased vertical integration, improved supply chains, and better margins.

Solid State Batteries

The CEO described them as “nearly solid state.” This has been a project at Electrovaya for some time, with at least three research lines followed. The latest cells, made on the bench, meet the company's expectations, and Electrovaya is investing in dry rooms and production facilities to produce the cells for customer evaluation.

These new cells will have lithium metal anodes, a cycle life of around 300, and an energy density in the range of 400-500 Wh/kg.

Unlike the technological advancements on the Infinity batteries, all of this R&D is being paid for by Electrovaya.

With each iteration of the solid-state battery, Electrovaya has changed the target market. It was initially being developed for EVs. When I spoke to the CEO last year, he was targeting consumer electronics, but he is now looking at the drone industry as the likely user of the batteries, and initial samples will be sent to military drone manufacturers.

Manufacturing

Electrovaya’s main facility is in Mississauga, Canada. They have recently expanded manufacturing there to a second shift to meet demand, and the CEO confirmed that this second shift will become a permanent fixture.

The second site in Jamestown, New York, is making good progress. Battery assembly at the site has begun ahead of schedule, and cell production will start at the site in mid-2026, ahead of a full-scale up in 2027.

It means from 2026, Electrovaya batteries will be made entirely in America. Few companies targeting their chosen vertical markets can say this, and it will likely be a big selling point with end users and necessary conditions for the military/government.

Finances

Electrovaya’s financial position continues to improve; revenue was up 67% in Q3 over the 2024 figure, and the company gave guidance of “greater than $60 million,” a marginal change from the previous guidance of $60m.

The CFO said the company's break-even point is $50 million in revenue, and they have reported $55 million in the previous 12 months and have received orders of $65 million.

Operating profit for the previous nine months was $33 million, an incredible increase from the $19,000 reported this time last year.

Electrovaya has now delivered two consecutive quarters of net profit, a significant improvement that enables me to forecast cash flow positive operations from mid-2026.

Gross margin is stabilizing at 31%, slightly above my long-term forecast

I have adjusted my mathematical model using the information released since I published it in my March article.

I have tightened the revenue guidance range, moving the conservative case higher. Increased the gross margin figures and altered the costs to show increased R&D spend but lower forecast sales, general and admin. I have also changed the working capital schedule in line with guidance from the CFO in the latest earnings call.

The new figures are:

Although the changes are minor, they have increased the forecast for EBIT by 2-3 million dollars per year. And the free cash flow figures increase more with the improvements to the working capital schedule.

When working out a fair value for the shares, I have to discount future cash flow, which requires key assumptions: the discounting rate and the terminal growth value.

In my earlier model, I used a discount rate of 8% and a terminal growth value of 0%. It produced a value of $9.60. Using those values today produces a fair value of $11.40.

I ran the model for several different combinations of discount rate and terminal value. The results are as follows.

New Fair Value

The number of new verticals and the pull-through demand suggest I should add a terminal growth rate greater than zero, and I have moved to 0.5%. It does add a little more risk, so I have increased the discounting rate to 10%.

It gives a new fair value of $14, and I will set that as my long-term target (at least until January, when I will review the model again)

Risks

This is a very bullish report. Electrovaya is delivering on all of its promises with growing revenue, improving bottom line, and expanding production facilities. The new verticals are very promising, and their main customers continue to order in significant volumes.

However, Electrovaya is still a small company with a niche product; it has a technological advantage at the moment and is able to charge a premium for the increased life cycle and safety profile it has been able to demonstrate for its batteries.

Other companies can and probably will catch up; it will reduce Electrovaya's ability to charge a premium price unless it has developed a strong brand name with its customers.

Electrovaya's revenue remains dependent on its big five customers; a slowdown in ordering from any of them would have a big impact on results. A slowdown in consumer spending would likely be the catalyst for these big customers to pull back on investments.

The new verticals are promising but not yet proven, and the New York manufacturing site is yet to produce any cells or approach scaled production.

The risks are real and they are large, which probably explains the considerable potential upside to owning this stock.

Conclusion

I have been following Electrovaya for several years, and it has been a very profitable investment. I have bought four times; my average price is $2.66, showing a 70% return.

The momentum of the business is accelerating, profitability has arrived, revenue is growing, and new verticals are opening.

Electrovaya continues to innovate with its existing market-leading Infinity range, developing a new, improved ceramic separator that new patents will protect. The new separator will increase the spec of the batteries and reduce the cost of manufacture.

The solid-state battery provides some long-term upside, and as Electrovaya has proven its ability to bring a new battery to market, manufacture it in volume, and sell it with high margins, I give it a relatively high chance of success.

Electrovaya remains a small emerging battery technology company, but unlike its peers, it has volume sales, scaled manufacturing, and profitable operations.

I reaffirm my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: AbbVie Is A Top Biotech Pick
data.description:
AbbVie Inc. (NYSE:ABBV) seems to have bucked the trend of health care weakness quite well. In the below chart, you can see that the stock has advanced around 18% YTD. Furthermore, AbbVie has fully recovered from Liberation Day losses and the stock price suggests that investors are optimistic on the company's outlook despite macro uncertainty. In the below analysis, it is shown that the company's fundamentals are strong. Furthermore, their commitment to U.S. manufacturing will likely put them in good position to mitigate tariff risk. Lastly, with their outlook being robust, I believe the valuation still has significant room for expansion and so I initiate the stock at a buy rating.

Net Revenues

AbbVie reported 2025 Q2 earnings at the end of July and top line results suggest a very strong business. The company was able to generate total revenues of $15.423 billion, up 6.6% on a reported basis or 6.5% on an operational basis. As you can see in the charts above, net revenues hit an all-time quarterly record, showing that the company is seeing impressive strength. In addition, despite a slight deceleration from the previous quarter, YoY growth remains at very strong levels relative to the past few years. AbbVie's top line also crushed expectations as they beat by a quite large margin of $392.71 million. From these revenue figures, it doesn't seem macro headwinds have had much effect on the company so far. Next, let's take a very brief look at their segment results.

Segment Results

Immunology

For their Immunology segment, they saw robust results as net revenues increased 9.5% on a reported basis or 9.2% on an operational basis to $7.631 billion. This is the largest of their segments and so it is nice to see that growth was healthy. With immunology, the largest driver for growth was Skyrizi which saw a 61.8% jump in revenues in operational terms. Note that Skyrizi is by far the largest contributor to revenues in this segment and so it is promising to see that it also had the highest growth.

Neuroscience

Neuroscience is their second largest segment and top line results were stellar. They saw a 24.2% increase in revenues on a reported basis and a 24% increase in operational terms to $2.683 billion. While Botox Therapeutic was the largest contributor to revenues, Ubrelvy and Qulipta were notable growth drivers. Vyalev saw the strongest growth with revenue increases north of 100% but it's contribution is minimal. However, this level of growth suggests that they likely have another strong growth driver for the years to come.

Oncology

Their oncology segment saw relatively sluggish results but still remained resilient overall. Net revenues grew 2.6% in reported terms and 2.4% in operational terms to $1.676 billion. The largest contributor to revenues, Imbruvica, saw declines of 9.5% on a operational basis. While this may be concerning for the near term, note that Elahere and Epkinly is gaining traction in adoption and so the future of this segment doesn't seem too concerning at this point.

Aesthetics

With their Aesthetics segment's products being a more discretionary category of items, I believe it's not that big of a surprise that it saw the weakest results among their major segments. Net revenues dropped 8.1% on a reported basis or 8.0% on an operational basis to $1.279 billion. Note that this is the smallest of their segments by revenue and so it is a positive that their weakest results came here in my view.

Margins

For their Q2, they reported an adjusted gross margin of 84.4%. That is actually a modest contraction of margin from 85.2% in the prior year period but I still wouldn't complain too much about the current figure. I would say that their cost control has been quite stable and it seems that their pricing power has remained strong. As for operating margin, they reported an adjusted figure of 44.3%, up notably from the year ago quarter's 42.6%. This of course shows an increase in corporate efficiency. If you look more closely at where this margin expansion comes from, it is even more encouraging. In the above chart, metrics for R&D expenses are shown. In 2024 Q2, adjusted R&D expenses were 13.3% of revenues. In 2025 Q2, it increased to 13.7% of revenues and so the company is increasing their spending for a brighter future. On the other hand, adjusted SG&A expenses were 22.9% of revenues in the prior year period versus just 21.0% in this year's Q2. Therefore, the increase in corporate efficiency came in the right places and does not come at the expense of R&D efforts.

Recent Events

Before moving on to their outlook, I want also briefly touch on some of their recent developments. Not long ago, the FDA approved another use of Rinvoq which will be the "first oral Janus Kinase (JAK) inhibitor approved for the treatment of adults with giant cell arteritis (GCA)." This is of course a positive development as this potentially increases the market size and will likely drive even stronger growth in that medication. In addition, just very recently on Aug 21st, AbbVie reported that they have succeeded in a late-stage trial of using Rinvoq to treat severe alopecia areata. This further adds to the growth potential of their existing medication.

Previously, it was reported that AbbVie would commit over $10 billion to expand their U.S. manufacturing. This has been an important development since the Trump administration has been continuously threatening the pharmaceutical industry with tariffs. A little while ago on Aug 12th, the company announced a $195 million investment to build a plant in Illinois. I believe this further demonstrates AbbVie's strong commitment to manufacturing in the U.S. and that should help them to mitigate tariff headwinds successfully moving forward.

EPS & Outlook

For Q2, AbbVie reported an adjusted EPS of $2.97, representing growth of 12.08% YoY. As you can see in the above chart, that is an acceleration of growth from the previous quarter's 6.49%. This EPS figure also beat expectations by $0.06 to show outperformance. For the full year, the company raised their guidance range from $11.67 - $11.87 to $11.88 - $12.08. At the midpoint, this represents growth of 18.38%. Note that in 2024, they had an EPS decline of 8.9% from 2023 levels and so 2025 is shaping up to be an incredible improvement from the previous year. While they did not provide specific guidance for Q3 and Q4, consensus EPS estimates for the next two quarters are shown above. For Q3 EPS growth is expected to moderate slightly to 9.13% YoY but for Q4, investors can expect a major acceleration of growth. Looking even further out to 2026, it seems that the outlook for AbbVie is robust.

Valuation Has Room To Expand

AbbVie's forward P/E ratio has expanded recently to a level that's above the historic average. However, its current reading of 17.47 is still far below 2024 highs and I believe it shows that the stock remains undervalued. As stated just above, EPS growth accelerated from Q1 to Q2's 12.08%. Furthermore, 2025 is now expected to be a much stronger year than 2024 and the outlook for the next four quarters looks strong according to analyst estimates. Another reason for optimism is the company's growing commitment to investing in U.S. manufacturing. This should help position AbbVie to successfully navigate the uncertainty in tariff policy. Due to these reasons, I believe the forward P/E ratio still has room for expansion and should probably be at multiyear highs. While the valuation has inched higher recently, it's likely not too late to buy in at current levels. Lastly, note that the Health Care sector's median non-GAAP forward P/E is at 18.05 currently. Therefore, according to this measure, AbbVie may still be at a discount while being one of the rare strong performers in the sector. This is another reason that I believe the valuation provides opportunity.

Conclusion

While the rest of the Health Care sector seems to be experiencing some headwinds, AbbVie is firing on all cylinders. Top line growth is robust, operating margin is expanding and as stated above, commitment to U.S. manufacturing highly offsets tariff headwinds. Looking ahead, EPS growth is expected to accelerate in Q4 and remain strong and so at this point, the outlook for the company is very bright. While the valuation has expanded a bit recently, the forward P/E ratio remains below 2024 highs and also below the Health Care sector's median. As a result, I conclude that the valuation continues to provide opportunity due to an attractive risk/reward setup. Therefore, a buy rating is justified in my view.
